text: The surgeon begins the cystectomy by making an incision in the peritoneum at the Douglas pouch.
The surgeon uses sharp and blunt dissection techniques to separate the seminal vesicles from the denonvilliers fascia, carefully revealing these anatomical structures.
The surgeon incises the Denonvilliers fascia and dissects the posterior surface of the prostate from the surrounding perirectal tissue.
The surgeon isolates and cuts the vas deferens on both sides, separating these tubular structures from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues to dissect the seminal vesicles from the surrounding tissue, progressing towards their junction with the prostate and posterior bladder neck.
The surgeon isolates the ureters, carefully separating them from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon excises the peritoneum over the iliac artery, revealing the underlying vascular structure.
The surgeon carefully dissects the ureter from the surrounding tissue, isolating it from adjacent anatomical structures.
The surgeon carefully preserves the periurethral tissue during dissection to prevent ischemia, ensuring minimal disturbance to the surrounding structures.
The surgeon continues dissecting the ureter until reaching the uretero-vesicle junction, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon clips the ureter twice and then transects it, completing the isolation process.
A frozen section is taken from the distal end of the dissected ureter and sent to pathology for analysis.
The surgeon performs the same dissection procedure on the opposite ureter, carefully separating it from surrounding tissue while preserving periureteral tissue.
The surgeon extends the incision along the lateral border of the median umbilical ligament, revealing underlying structures.
The surgeon uses sharp and blunt dissection techniques to separate the lateral wall of the bladder from the pelvic side wall, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the procedure.
The surgeon clips the umbilical artery twice at its origin and transects it using a ligature.
One may certainly use other sources of energy or cold cutting after clipping.
But we prefer to minimize the risk of bleeding and use ligature.
The surgeon continues the dissection, controlling the pedicle by repeatedly applying the LigaSure device and performing sharp dissection with scissors.
The surgeon completes the dissection, freeing the prostate and bladder from the rectum. The retzius space is dissected anteriorly, and the bladder is visibly dropped from the abdominal wall.
The dissection sequence is performed to prevent the early posterior dropping of the bladder, enhancing exposure for the subsequent posterior dissection.
The Alexis port, located at the stoma site, is visible in the upper right corner of the screen, indicating its use for safe insufflation during the procedure.
This will be further used during the creation of urinary diversion.
The surgeon dissects the periprostatic fatty tissue, revealing the dorsal venous complex beneath.
The surgeon performs sharp dissection of the dorsal venous complex, with minimal bleeding observed due to the pneumoperitoneum pressure.
The surgeon achieves vascular control of the dorsal venous complex (DVC) using an A-III-Zero monofilament suture ligature after its complete transection.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the prostatic apex, fully exposing the urethra. The urethra is then clipped twice before being transected.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the surgical field to prevent any spillage of urine during the procedure.
A frozen section is obtained from the apical urethra and sent for analysis, despite the planned diversion being an ileal loop.
If the frozen section comes back as tumor positive, we then perform a complete urethrectomy.
The surgeon places the dissected specimen into a surgical retrieval bag for removal from the surgical field.
The surgeon removes the specimen through a small phantasteel incision, following the dissection and clipping of the urethra.
The surgical team leaves the specimen bag within the abdominal cavity, opting to retrieve it at the conclusion of the procedure.
The surgeon secures the tight closure of the specimen bag with a clip or other means to prevent spillage within the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon transfers the left ureter beneath the colon to the right side. A segment of ileum, approximately twenty centimeters from the ileocecal valve, is marked and divided using surgical staplers.
The surgeon restores the continuity of the ileum by performing a side-to-side anastomosis using two fires of 60mm staplers, aligning and stapling the ileal segments together.
The surgeon uses a stapler to close the side opening of the ileum anastomosis, completing the restoration of intestinal continuity.
The surgeon opens and spatulates the left ureter, preparing it for anastomosis.
An incision is made at the lateral border of the ileum at a location suitable for uretero-ileal anastomosis.

The surgeon performs an anastomosis of the ureter to the ileum using a novel technique, with the ureter positioned on the right side beneath the colon and the ileum segment marked and divided approximately twenty centimeters from the ileocecal valve.
The surgeon uses a 4-0 PDS suture to secure the ureter to the ileal wall, ensuring proper fixation of the uretero-ileal anastomosis.
The surgeon uses the Alexis port to exteriorize the distal end of the isolated ileal segment from the body.
A metal suction cannula is inserted into the ileum and is visibly brought out through the proximal ileal incision.
Guided by the metal suction cannula, the surgeon inserts a ureter catheter loaded on a guide wire into the ureter.
The ureter catheters are visibly exiting through the ileal stoma, facilitated by the novel anastomosis technique, without requiring additional punctures in the ileal or abdominal wall.
Since there are no puncture sites at the ileal wall, the risk of urine leakage from these sites is also avoided.
The surgeon secures the ureteral catheter to the ileum using A-V-O rapid vicryl sutures, ensuring the catheter remains in place within the isolated ileal segment.
We prefer this method over fixing the catheter with a suture through the ureter, since ureter is more delicate, and any injury to the ureteric tissue is best avoided.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis using continuous sutures on both sides, employing four OPDS sutures to secure the connection.
The surgeon repeats the anastomosis procedure for the right ureter, using continuous sutures to connect the ureter to the ileum without puncturing the ileal wall.
The surgeon completes the ureter of ileal anastomosis on both sides, then brings the distal end of the ileum out of the abdomen through the Alexis port and matures it into a stoma.
This novel method, reduces the time for anastomosis and ileal stoma creation, by eliminating the clumsy manipulation of guide wires or ureter catheters in a floppy ileum.
It also avoids further risk of urine leakage, which may be a concern if the catheters are brought out by stab wounds through ileum and abdominal wall.
